text: The surgeon performs a complete mesocolic excision in a right hemicolectomy using a medial to lateral approach. Trochars are placed, and the robot is docked from the right side of the patient. The great omentum and transverse colon are positioned in the upper abdomen. The pancreas and duodenum are visible, and the iliocolic vein and superior mesenteric vein are identified. The small intestine is moved to the left side of the abdomen using a sponge.

The surgeon performs a complete mesocolic excision in a right hemicolectomy using a medial to lateral approach. Trochars are placed, and the robot is docked from the right side of the patient. The great omentum and transverse colon are moved to the upper abdomen, revealing the pancreas and duodenum. The iliocolic vein and superior mesenteric vein are identified, while the small intestine is positioned to the left side of the abdomen using a sponge.

The surgeon incises the visceral peritoneum above the superior mesenteric vein using monopolar scissors. The ileocolic vein is identified and surrounded completely before clipping. The incision is extended to reduce tension, and the robotic end of wrist aids in achieving the necessary angulation for clipping the ileocolic vein close to the trunk of the superior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon begins preparation by incising the visceral peritoneum above the superior mesenteric vein using monopolar scissors. The ileocolic vein is identified and surrounded completely before clipping. The incision is extended to reduce tension, and the robotic end of wrist aids in angulating the instruments for precise clipping close to the trunk of the superior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon surrounds and mobilizes the right colic vein along the superior mesenteric vein in a cranial direction. The ileocolic artery is identified behind the stump of the ileocolic vein and is surrounded completely before clipping. Monopolar energy and sharp dissection are used to remove adherent tissue from the vessels. The artery is clipped sharply at the trunk of the superior mesenteric artery using a laparoscopic clip applier, with two clips placed on the patient side and one on the specimen side.

The surgeon surrounds the right colic vein and continues mobilization along the superior mesenteric vein in a cranial direction. The ileocolic artery is identified behind the stump of the ileocolic vein and surrounded before clipping. Monopolar energy and sharp dissection are used to remove adherent tissue from the vessels. The artery is clipped sharply at the trunk of the superior mesenteric artery using a laparoscopic clip applier. Two clips are placed on the patient side and one on the specimen side. After cutting the artery, preparation continues towards the duodenum and pancreas behind the dorsal aspect of the ascending mesocolon. The small right colic vessels are clipped as one package, with care taken to avoid pancreatic injury.

The surgeon continues preparation towards the duodenum and pancreas, working behind the dorsal aspect of the ascending mesocolon. The small right colic vessels are surrounded and clipped as a single package. The avascular layer between the mesopancreas and ascending mesocolon is identified, and preparation proceeds along this plane.

The surgeon identifies the avascular layer between the mesopancreas and ascending mesocolon, continuing preparation along this plane. The pancreas and duodenum are protected while the mesocolon is lifted using the robot's second arm. The superior mesenteric vein and artery serve as medial landmarks during this process.

The surgeon uses the second arm of the robot to lift the mesocolon, protecting the pancreas and duodenum. The superior mesenteric vein and artery serve as medial landmarks. A sponge is used to prevent damage to the ascending mesocolon. Monopolar energy is applied cautiously along the duodenum to avoid serosal lesions. During central dissection, the Henle trunk is identified, along with the middle colic artery and its right and left branches. The transverse mesocolon is lifted, revealing the superior pancreatic vein and the right gastroepiploic vein, which are left intact. The right branch of the middle colic artery is dissected, clipped, and divided, utilizing the robot's inner wrist for precise maneuvers.

The surgeon uses a sponge to protect the ascending mesocolon during preparation along the duodenum. Monopolar energy is applied cautiously to avoid serosal lesions. The Henle trunk is identified along the central dissection of the superior mesenteric vein. The middle colic artery with its right and left branches is visible. As the transverse mesocolon is lifted, the superior pancreatic vein and right gastroepiploic vein are observed but left intact. The right branch of the middle colic artery is dissected, clipped, and divided, utilizing the robotic inner wrist for precise maneuvers.

A sponge is placed between the ascending mesocolon and the pancreatic head. The transverse colon is pulled down, and the greater omentum is mobilized from the transverse colon, providing access to the lesser sac. The complete Henle trunk with all draining veins is clearly visible. The right colon flexure is fully mobilized, and the transverse colon is lifted to the upper abdomen. Dissection continues distally along the mesenteric vessels, with sharp dissection in the avascular layer between parietal and visceral fascia. The ascending mesocolon is lifted to the ventral side using the second robotic arm.

A sponge is placed between the ascending mesocolon and pancreatic head. The transverse colon is pulled down, and the greater omentum is mobilized from the transverse colon, providing access to the lesser sac. The greater omentum is completely mobilized from the colon and the cranial portion of the mesocolon. The sponge placed from the medial side is visible, giving access to the prepared dissection plane. The complete Henle trunk with all draining veins is clearly visible. The right colon flexure is fully mobilized, and the transverse colon is lifted again to the upper abdomen. Dissection continues distally along the mesenteric vessels.

The surgeon performs sharp dissection in the avascular layer between the parietal and visceral fascia, using traction and counter traction to identify the correct space. The thin mesocolon is carefully lifted to the ventral side of the patient with the second robotic arm. The dissection of the mesocolon is completed towards the terminal ileum, and approximately ten centimeters of the distal ileum are removed during the right hemicolectomy.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the mesocolon towards the terminal ileum, removing approximately ten centimeters of the distal ileum during the right hemicolectomy. The colon is mobilized from the lateral abdominal wall with assistance pulling it medially. The right hemicolon is fully mobilized and extracted through a small supraumbilical incision, where an open end-to-end anastomosis is performed. The intact specimen displays a clear central portion of the dissected vessels.

The surgeon mobilizes the colon from the lateral abdominal wall, with the assistant pulling the colon medially. The right hemicolon is fully mobile and luxated through a small supraumbilical incision. The colon is removed, and an open end-to-end anastomosis is performed. The specimen is intact, displaying a central proportion of dissected vessels.
